artifacts.content:
From: alexandra.nguyen@riversidecbhc.org
To: priya.shah@ucdenver.edu
Subject: Burnout + $$ panic (again, sorry)
Date: 2022-02-10

Hey Priya,

Typing this from the Riverside parking lot because apparently that’s my new crying spot. Don’t worry, I’m okay-ish, just wrung out.

Clinic was a lot today—three new meth inductions, one guy I’ve followed for two years relapsed after his brother OD’d, and then a hallway “debate” with Klein about benzos that brought me right back to last November. Remember when he told me I was “overreacting” about the clonazepam scripts and “this is how we’ve always done it here”? I still have the email I sent our medical director with the subject line “Concern re: benzo co-prescribing and clinic risk exposure” sitting in my Sent folder like a little time bomb. Nothing really changed, except now he avoids eye contact.

The work stuff would be manageable if the money piece wasn’t so tight. Daniel’s contract gigs are all over the place, and I’m staring at my stupid “Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx” every weekend trying to make the FedLoan payment and the Elevations Credit Union personal loan both happen. I’m still sitting on about $185k in med school loans plus that $9.5k I took out in 2018 after the UCHealth scare, when I ended up in the ER at University of Colorado Hospital with chest pain/near-syncope from overwork and had to cut back my fellowship shifts for six weeks. It feels like I’m still paying for that one night.

I actually told Dr. Hart at my Denver Health telehealth session yesterday (the usual Wednesday afternoon “telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue” block on my calendar) that I’ve fantasized about quitting safety-net work and just doing part-time telepsych for some corporate chain to dig out of this hole. She didn’t freak out, just asked what it would mean for how I see myself. Cue more tears.

Anyway, I know you’re slammed too, but if you’re up for a Zoom or coffee sometime soon, I’d love to see your face and talk about literally anything other than RVUs and loan servicers.

Love,
Alex

Title: Running list – Michael care & guardianship logistics
Date: 2022-02-18

1. Guardianship paperwork
- Remember: formal legal guardianship for Michael since Sept 2021 – Denver Juvenile Court case starting “2021JG…”. I’m the one who signs for everything now.
- Need to scan the latest order and upload to shared family folder so school and doctors stop asking me to re-fax it.
- When I get overwhelmed, remind myself *why* this happened: Mom’s two hospitalizations at Penrose Hospital in Colorado Springs last summer/fall (heart failure + out-of-control diabetes, including that ICU stay). There really wasn’t another option.

2. Medical
- Michael’s chronic autoimmune thing – continue follow-up at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora.
- Next quarterly neurology visit with Dr. Aaron Feldman (peds neuro) – confirm date/time, make sure it doesn’t conflict with my Wednesday afternoon group.
- Keep the “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT” Google Sheet updated. Double-check daily dosing column on Sundays and refill dates so we don’t end up in pharmacy hell.
- Track out-of-pocket costs; it feels like “several hundred a month” in copays/meds alone. Add to budget tab in Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.

3. School
- Email his counselor about IEP check-in; bring guardianship order and med list.
- Remind Michael (again) that I’m the legal decision-maker now, not because Mom and Dad don’t love him, but because they were drowning when Penrose discharged her the second time.

4. Emotional stuff
- He’s still mad about moving from Colorado Springs to Denver. Validate that, but also be honest about Dad’s comment in January (“You’re choosing your job over this family”) and how impossible that ultimatum felt.
- Maybe bring this up with Dr. Hart at next compassion-fatigue session; balance between being his guardian and his sister is blurry.

5. Practical
- Color-code his appointments on the family Google Calendar so Daniel knows when he has to cover Sophie pickup.
- Pack snacks + headphones for the long Aurora clinic days.

metadata.title: Running list – Michael care & guardianship logistics

artifacts.content:
Event: Telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue
Date: Wednesday, 2022-01-26
Time: 4:15–5:00 p.m.
Location: Home office (corner of bedroom), Zoom – Denver Health Outpatient Behavioral Health Clinic
Attendees: Alexandra Nguyen, MD; Melissa Hart, PhD

Notes:
- Monthly therapy – started late 2020 after that run of overdoses + feeling numb in clinic.
- Agenda for today:
  • Update on Riverside workload – MOUD expansion, more meth cases, fallout from November 2021 incident w/ Dr. Klein (he told me in the hallway, “You’re overreacting; this is how we’ve always done it here,” when I raised concerns about unsafe benzo co-prescribing). I filed the internal incident report and emailed our medical director (“Concern re: benzo co-prescribing and clinic risk exposure”), but tension still lingers.
  • Talk through ongoing “Burnout + $$ panic” loop – Daniel’s unstable contract income, my loan situation (approx. $185k federal med school loans w/ FedLoan + $9.5k Elevations CU personal loan from Oct 2018 when I reduced hours after UCHealth overnight chest-pain workup). Mention how I obsess over the “Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx” spreadsheet.
  • Check in about guardianship stress since Sept 2021 Denver Juvenile Court order making me Michael’s legal guardian. Juggle between his Children’s Hospital Colorado neurology appointments with Dr. Feldman, meds tracked in “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT,” and Mom’s health at Penrose.
  • Process January phone fight w/ Dad in their Colorado Springs kitchen (“You’re choosing your job over this family”) when I said I couldn’t move in and quit Riverside to be full-time caregiver.

Reminders:
- Ask Dr. Hart for concrete boundary strategies (email curfew, no opening EMR after 9 p.m.).
- Bring up occasional fantasy about quitting safety-net work for corporate telepsych and whether that’s avoidance vs. realistic option.
- Log in 5 minutes early to test audio so I’m not flustered.

metadata.title: Telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue
metadata.location: Home / Zoom – Denver Health Outpatient Behavioral Health Clinic

artifacts.content:
From: alexandra.nguyen@riversidecbhc.org
To: linh.tran@gmail.com
Subject: Can we steal you for Saturday?
Date: 2022-03-02

Hi Linh,

Is there any chance you’re free this Saturday afternoon to hang with Sophie and Michael for a few hours? Daniel picked up a last-minute event gig at the convention center, and I’m trying to squeeze in a real grocery run plus maybe 30 minutes of not being “Dr. Nguyen” or “Mom” or “legal guardian” to anyone.

Michael has his next neurology follow-up with Dr. Feldman at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora later this month, and I need to sit down with the “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT” sheet and make sure we’re not missing any labs before we go. His dosing schedule has gotten more complicated, and between that and everything with Mom after her Penrose ICU stays, my brain is mush.

On top of that, I spent half of last night staring at my stupid loan spreadsheet (the infamous Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx) instead of sleeping. I keep running the numbers on the ~$185k in federal loans and the $9.5k personal loan from Elevations I took out back in 2018 after the UCHealth chest-pain scare forced me to cut my hours. It feels like no matter how many extra notes I finish at home, the balances don’t move.

Work has been its own soap opera. Since that November hallway thing with Dr. Klein (“You’re overreacting; this is how we’ve always done it here”), it’s been awkward. I did the right thing filing the incident report and emailing our medical director about the benzo co-prescribing, but being the squeaky wheel is exhausting.

Anyway, if you’re free 1–4 p.m., I can drop the kids at your place in Aurora on my way to Costco. I’ll bring banh mi from New Saigon as a bribe. Sophie will want to show you her new dinosaur stickers, and Michael mostly just needs Wi-Fi and snacks.

Love,
Alex

metadata.subject: Can we steal you for Saturday?

artifacts.content:
Thread: Alex & Dad
Date: 2022-01-10

Bao: We need to talk about yesterday.

Alexandra: I know, Ba. I’m still at Riverside finishing notes but I can text a little.

Bao: Your mother was crying all night. She heard you say you cannot come to Colorado Springs to stay. She thinks you don’t care.

Alexandra: I do care. That’s why I’ve been driving down after clinic, juggling Michael’s appointments, everything with Penrose after her ICU stays. I just can’t quit my job and move in full-time.

Bao: You are choosing your job over this family. I said that in the kitchen yesterday and I mean it.

Alexandra: That’s not fair. Since September, I’ve been Michael’s legal guardian because of the Denver Juvenile Court order. All the decisions for him are on me now. I have to keep my income and my insurance for his care at Children’s in Aurora and Mom’s meds too.

Bao: When we were your age, we didn’t think like this. Family first.

Alexandra: Family first is why I took guardianship when you both were overwhelmed after Mom’s second hospitalization at Penrose. It’s why I track Michael’s meds in that Google Sheet and take him to see Dr. Feldman every few months. It’s why I sit in therapy with Dr. Hart at Denver Health talking about compassion fatigue so I don’t burn out and disappear on everyone.

Bao: Therapy again? You are strong, you don’t need that.

Alexandra: I need it, Ba. Last time I ignored stress I ended up in the UCHealth ER in 2019 with chest pain and had to cut my hours. I’m trying not to repeat that.

Bao: …We talk more when you come this weekend.

Alexandra: Okay. I’ll bring Sophie. Please tell Mom I love her.

Title: Money + work brain dump (late-night)
Date: 2022-02-22

Couldn’t sleep again, so trying to get this out of my head instead of scrolling.

1. Debt snapshot
- ~185,000 in federal med school loans (FedLoan) + 9,500 personal loan from Elevations Credit Union (took it Oct 2018 when I reduced hours after the UCHealth University of Colorado Hospital overnight chest-pain/near-syncope workup on the inpatient psych unit). Still feels surreal that one episode = years of payments.
- Keep updating Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx every Sunday. Color coding helps but also makes it obvious how tight things are.

2. Work stress
- Since November 2021, still thinking about the incident with Dr. Robert Klein at Riverside. Hallway outside Exam 7: I raised concerns about unsafe benzo co-prescribing, he said, “You’re overreacting; this is how we’ve always done it here.” I went back to my office shaking, then wrote that email to our medical director (“Concern re: benzo co-prescribing and clinic risk exposure”) and filed the incident report. Proud I did it, but also worried I’ve branded myself as difficult.

3. Burnout signs
- Parking lot crying spells after clinic, then emailing Priya from my Riverside account with subject lines like “Burnout + $$ panic.” Not my finest boundary moment, but she gets it.
- Wednesday afternoon therapy with Dr. Melissa Hart at Denver Health’s Outpatient Behavioral Health Clinic (calendar label: “telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue”) has been a lifeline. Talked last week about the fantasy of quitting safety-net work for a corporate telepsych chain just to make the numbers less terrifying.

4. Family overlay
- Michael’s guardianship (Denver Juvenile Court 2021JG…) and his quarterly Children’s Hospital Colorado neurology visits with Dr. Feldman, plus meds tracked in “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT,” add time and money stress.
- Dad’s “You’re choosing your job over this family” line from their Colorado Springs kitchen keeps echoing.

Goal: bring this whole tangled mess to Dr. Hart next session instead of pretending I’m fine.

From: alexandra.nguyen@riversidecbhc.org
To: self (personal)
Subject: Things I don’t want to forget to mention to Dr. Hart
Date: 2022-03-09

Alex,

Quick brain dump before you lose track of all this.

1. Work
- Talk about how the clinic still feels different after the November 2021 hallway run-in with Dr. Klein (“You’re overreacting; this is how we’ve always done it here”) and the email/incident report (“Concern re: benzo co-prescribing and clinic risk exposure”). You’re still carrying that tension into every team meeting.

2. Old health scare
- Revisit August 2019 at UCHealth University of Colorado Hospital – chest pain + near-syncope on the inpatient psych unit, overnight cardiac workup, then six-week reduction in fellowship hours. You keep minimizing it, but it’s the reason for the Elevations Credit Union loan and some of the current anxiety about “breaking” again.

3. Money spiral
- Be honest about how much time you spend staring at Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx, re-running the ~185k FedLoan balance + 9.5k Elevations loan numbers. The late-night catastrophizing is getting worse.

4. Guardianship + family
- Since Sept 2021 Denver Juvenile Court guardianship order (2021JG…), you’re juggling Michael’s care: Children’s Hospital Colorado neurology with Dr. Feldman, meds tracked in “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT,” school IEPs.
- Layer on Mom’s Penrose Hospital admissions and Dad’s January kitchen ultimatum in Colorado Springs (“You’re choosing your job over this family”). That sentence is living rent-free in your head.

5. Parking lot moments
- Those evenings you sit in the Riverside parking lot after clinic, crying, then emailing Priya with subject lines like “Burnout + $$ panic” and wondering if you should “just quit and go part-time telepsychiatry for a corporate chain” to keep up with bills.

Bring this to the next Wednesday “telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue” so you don’t spend 20 minutes saying, “Everything’s fine, just busy.”

– A

metadata.subject: Things I don’t want to forget to mention to Dr. Hart

artifacts.content:
Hi Linh,

I hope you're doing well! Things have been quite busy on my end, and I wanted to update you on some developments, both personal and professional.

First, I’m planning to attend the 2022 AAAP Annual Meeting soon. It's been a while since I last attended—back in 2019 in San Diego. I also went to a regional meeting in Denver in October 2021. These meetings are such valuable opportunities to learn and grow, especially with the focus on addiction psychiatry, which is my passion. This year, the registration, airfare, lodging, and dues will total up to $1,605.00. It's quite a sum, especially with our current financial situation. As you know, Daniel’s contract work has been unpredictable since his layoff, and supporting Michael has added to our expenses. I’m applying for assistance through the AAAP's Member Assistance Program to help cover these costs. Balancing everything has been a bit of a challenge lately, but I’m hopeful we’ll get through it.

At Riverside, my work continues to be both challenging and rewarding. Our clinic has seen a surge in patients with substance use disorders, particularly opioids and methamphetamines. I lead a team focused on expanding access to medications for opioid use disorder, and we’re piloting bilingual relapse-prevention programs. It’s important work, but it can be heavy, especially when dealing with patients who face homelessness or have legal issues.

On the home front, Sophie is growing up so fast—she’s already four! Michael seems to be doing better, though managing his care is almost a second job. It's been good having you nearby for support—thank you for all the times you've helped with Sophie. Hopefully, we can plan a day to take the kids to the Denver Zoo or even down to visit Mom and Dad in Colorado Springs. It would be nice to have a break and catch up properly.

Lastly, I’m still in touch with some colleagues from residency, and we're trying to arrange a coffee meetup soon. It's always good to connect and share our experiences in this demanding field. I miss those get-togethers and hope to have more time for them in the future.

Looking forward to hearing from you soon.

Take care,

Alexandra
